title: Share of people who trust their national government
subtitle: Share of respondents who answered "a lot" or "some" to the question: "How much do you trust your national government?"
caption:
Share of people who trust their national government

Share of respondents who answered "a lot" or "some" to the question: "How much do you trust your national government?"

This choropleth world map displays the share of people who trust their national government in 2020, based on survey responses. The legend ranges from 0% to 100%, with color bands marking 10-point intervals from light lavender (0–10%) to dark navy blue (90–100%). Countries shaded in the darkest band — indicating 90% or higher trust — include Vietnam and Rwanda. Several countries fall into the 80–90% range, such as India, Indonesia, and Australia. In North America, Canada appears in the 70–80% band, while the United States sits in the 60–70% range. Across Europe, Norway and Finland are in the 70–80% band; Germany and France appear in the 50–60% range; and Italy and Spain sit in the 40–50% band. Russia is shaded in the 60–70% range. In Latin America, Brazil and Argentina fall in the 30–40% band, while Uruguay appears slightly darker, near 50%. Many countries in Africa show variation: Egypt and Ethiopia are in the 70–80% band, while South Africa and Kenya sit in the 50–60% range. Large portions of the Middle East and Central Asia lack data, shown with diagonal hatching — including Saudi Arabia, Iran, and Kazakhstan. Greenland and Antarctica also display no data. The visual pattern shows higher trust levels concentrated in parts of Southeast Asia, Oceania, and select African nations, while lower trust levels appear across much of Latin America and Southern Europe.
